slides.description: This is the first slide, titled "AI Screenplays" in large, bold blue text at the top. The main image is a detailed cartoon illustration in the style of South Park, depicting a lavish office environment. A character resembling a grey-haired Hollywood executive, wearing a black suit and red tie, stands on the right, gesturing towards a large window that overlooks a cityscape with the "HOLLYWOOD" sign visible on the hills. Next to him, a large cardboard robot labeled "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" (a reference to Cartman's robot disguise from South Park) sits on a chair. The office is decorated with film awards like Oscars and Emmys on a dark wooden desk, a fish tank with an alien figure, a globe, and a framed family photo. A notable framed painting on the wall above the fish tank reads "AWESOM-O" with "LAME" stamped over it, suggesting a critical or humorous take on the subject of AI screenplays.
slides.narration: Alright, everyone, let's get the party started! So, yo, I'm your instructor, and welcome to what I think is a super exciting topic: "AI Screenplays." You know, like, the dream of having an AI just churn out a whole movie script? Pretty cool, right? But also, uh, if you look at our little Awesom-O robot here, and that 'LAME' stamp, it hints at something. See, while AI can do a lot of amazing things, getting it to write a *coherent*, *long-form* document like a screenplay in one go, without it totally losing the plot, is actually incredibly challenging. Today, we're gonna dive into *why* that's hard and, more importantly, *how* we can build a super powerful, multi-agent system to make it happen. It's gonna be awesome.

slides.description: This slide, titled "Generating Long Documents" in large bold blue text, transitions from the previous slide's humorous introduction to AI screenplays. It features four bullet points on a light grey background, detailing the core subject. The first bullet states "AI can be used to generate long, coherent text documents," followed by examples like "Books, reports, screenplays." The third bullet point announces, "Today we will build an agentic flow to generate screenplays for movies," directly linking back to the previous slide's theme. Finally, it notes that "This flow architecture can be generalized to other long text documents," indicating the broader applicability of the discussed method beyond screenplays. The slide sets the practical agenda for the lecture, moving from the conceptual and illustrative to the technical application of AI in document generation.
slides.narration: Okay, so, picking up from our Awesom-O robot, the *good* news is, AI *can* actually be used to generate really long, coherent text documents. I mean, we're talking about serious stuff here, like books, detailed reports, and, yeah, those screenplays we were just kinda poking fun at. Today, folks, we're gonna dive in and actually *build* an agentic flow, step-by-step, specifically to generate screenplays for movies. And here's the super cool part: this exact flow architecture can be generalized to all sorts of other long text documents. Pretty awesome, right?

slides.description: This slide is titled "Challenge: "One-Shot" Long-Form" in large, bold blue text, set against a light grey background. It outlines the difficulties encountered when using AI to generate extensive, coherent documents. The first bullet point states that it is challenging for an AI to generate a long coherent document in one-shot. The second main point highlights the "Local vs global" issue, explaining that AI is proficient at writing smaller sections but struggles to maintain coherence throughout an entire document. This is further elaborated with two sub-bullets: the AI can become "lost in the middle" and experience "context drift," potentially leading to hallucinations or a loss of focus deep within the text. This slide transitions from the previous one's announcement of generating long documents by immediately addressing the core technical challenge that the proposed "agentic flow" (mentioned in Slide 2) is intended to overcome.
slides.narration: Okay, so we just talked about how cool it is that we *can* generate long documents with AI, right? But here's the big, like, *actual* challenge. It's really, really difficult for an AI to just spit out a super long, totally coherent document in one shot. And the main reason is this whole 'local versus global' problem. The AI is actually pretty stellar at writing small, self-contained parts, you know, like a paragraph or a scene. But asking it to maintain that coherence and focus across a *whole* movie screenplay? That's where it gets 'lost in the middle.' You get 'context drift,' where deep into the document, the AI kinda starts to hallucinate or, like, totally loses focus of what it's supposed to be writing about. It's a real pain, honestly.

slides.description: This slide is titled "Movie Screenplays" in large, bold blue text, set against a light grey background. It details the essential elements and structure an AI needs to understand when generating screenplays. The first main bullet point highlights that "Screenplays have many different elements the AI needs to capture," listing sub-points like "Story shape," "Goals of 'sequences' in the movie," and "Scene action and dialogue." The second major bullet discusses the "Hierarchical structure of a screenplay," breaking it down into increasingly granular components: "Premise – basic elements of the movie," "Story arc – high level plot structure," "Sequences – more detailed plot," and "Scenes – even more detailed plot." This slide directly follows the discussion of challenges in generating long-form content by elaborating on the complexity of screenplays, suggesting that understanding this hierarchical structure is key to overcoming issues like 'context drift' and 'local vs global' coherence, which were introduced in Slide 3.
slides.narration: Okay, so we just talked about how difficult it is for AI to do 'one-shot' long-form stuff, right? And that context drift? It's a real buzzkill, honestly. Well, movie screenplays are, like, the perfect example of why this is such a pain if you don't break it down. They have *so* many different elements the AI needs to capture, you know? It's not just, 'write a story,' right? It's about the overall story shape, the goals of specific 'sequences' – which are basically mini-arcs within the acts – and then, you know, the actual scene action and dialogue. And here's the kicker, folks, the key to solving this is recognizing the *inherent hierarchical structure* of a screenplay. You start with a really high-level Premise, which are the basic elements of the movie. Then you move to the Story Arc, that's your high-level plot structure across acts. Then you get into Sequences, which are more detailed plot points. And finally, Scenes – like, the really, really detailed plot, action, and dialogue. See how we're breaking it down?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Screenplay Flow" in large bold blue text, presents a visual diagram illustrating the hierarchical, multi-step process for generating a screenplay. Set against a dark blue background, the diagram shows four distinct rectangular nodes, each with a small cartoon box icon and a corresponding .json file name. The flow begins with a blue "Premise" node (premise.json), which leads via an arrow to a purple "Arc" node (arc.json). From the "Arc" node, two separate arrows branch out: one pointing to an orange "Sequences" node (sequence.json) and another pointing down to a green "Scenes" node (scenes.json). This diagram directly visualizes the "agentic flow" for screenplay generation and the hierarchical structure of screenplays introduced in previous slides, offering a solution to the 'one-shot' long-form generation challenge by breaking it down into manageable, interconnected stages.
slides.narration: Okay, so, speaking of breaking it down, right? This, folks, this is our "Screenplay Flow." This is the visual representation of that hierarchical, multi-agent architecture we're gonna use to absolutely crush that whole context drift problem. See, we start with our really high-level Premise, which then outputs our `premise.json` file. That feeds directly into our Arc, giving us the `arc.json`. And then, from the Arc, we actually fan out to generate our more detailed Sequences, which is our `sequence.json`, and finally, the really, really granular individual Scenes, which become our `scenes.json`. This visual, I think, perfectly shows you exactly how we're gonna build that coherent, long-form screenplay, step-by-step. Pretty awesome, right?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Premise Agent" in large bold blue text, further elaborates on the first step of the "Screenplay Flow" diagram introduced in Slide 5. Against a light grey background, the slide details the function of this agent, which is a chat interface designed to discuss the core elements of a movie, such as its title, logline, characters, genre, and central conflict. It also highlights that users can prompt the agent for plot ideas if they are creatively stuck. The slide notes that this initial conversational process may take several minutes to define all major movie elements, ultimately leading to the generation of a "premise.json" file. A small, cropped image of the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot, a recurring visual from the first slide, is present in the top right corner, maintaining the lecture's thematic branding.
slides.narration: Okay, so, speaking of that "Premise" node in our flow, right? This, folks, this is our Premise Agent. This is actually a chat agent, and it's where you basically, like, download all your brilliant movie ideas into the system. You're gonna discuss with it what your movie is about: the title, the logline – which is kinda like your executive summary, you know? – your main characters, the genre, and of course, the central conflict. And hey, if you're totally stuck, you can even ask the agent to, like, give you some ideas for the plot. Pretty cool, right? This initial chat, it can take a few minutes, honestly, to really specify all those major elements. But it's super important, because once you're done, this agent is gonna create that crucial `premise.json` file for us. Awesome.

slides.description: This slide is titled "Premise.json" in large bold blue text, displaying the output file from the Premise Agent. The main content is a dark code block showcasing a JSON file with various movie elements. It includes a "project_id," a "title" of "Shadow of the Dark World," and "writers" listed as "TBD." Notably, "format_notes" state that "Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat," linking back to the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O character and the conversational agent. The "logline" describes a quest involving characters reminiscent of Link, Zelda, and Ganon, further enriched by "genre" tags like fantasy, action, and adventure, and "themes" such as 'hero's journey' and 'good versus evil.' This slide directly demonstrates the first tangible output of the "Premise Agent" discussed in Slide 6, fulfilling the creation of the 'premise.json' file introduced in the "Screenplay Flow" diagram on Slide 5.
slides.narration: Okay, so here it is, folks! This is our glorious `premise.json` file, the first actual output from our Premise Agent, right? See how it's got, like, the 'project_id' and the 'title' – 'Shadow of the Dark World.' And those 'format_notes'? It even says 'Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat,' which is pretty cool, you know? But look at that 'logline' – it's basically your entire movie in one sentence! And then we've got the 'genre' – fantasy, action, adventure – and even 'themes' like 'hero's journey' and 'destiny.' This is the foundational context for *everything* else we're gonna build. Pretty neat, huh?

slides.description: This slide is titled "Premise.json" in large, bold blue text, continuing the display of the JSON file generated by the Premise Agent. Against a light grey background, a dark code block presents a continuation of the `premise.json` file, picking up where the previous slide left off. It details the "central_conflict" of the movie, describing Link's quest to stop Aghanim and Ganon. The "setting" is further defined with a "primary_time" of "High fantasy era" and a list of "primary_places" including Hyrule Castle and Dark World. Finally, the "protagonists" section is introduced, specifically describing "Link" with his role as an "Unassuming hero" and his desire to save Zelda and prevent Hyrule's destruction. This slide further elaborates on the concrete output of the Premise Agent, showcasing the rich detail captured for the movie's core elements before moving to the next stage of screenplay generation.
slides.narration: Alright, so picking up right where we left off with our `premise.json` file, check out the 'central_conflict' here! It's like, Link's epic race against time, right? To stop Aghanim and Ganon from totally messing up the Light World. Pretty high stakes, bro! And then, look at the 'setting' we've got: 'High fantasy era' but with, like, a 'contemporary-feel for characters,' which is kind of cool, you know? And all these awesome 'primary_places' like Hyrule Castle and Dark World. And finally, we're diving into our 'protagonists,' starting with our main man, 'Link,' described as this 'Unassuming hero' destined to wield the Master Sword. You can already feel the story building, right?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Premise.json" in large bold blue text against a light grey background, continues to display the output of the Premise Agent. The dark code block shows the latter part of the JSON file, picking up from the protagonist's description of 'Link' and his motivations. It then introduces the 'key_antagonist' as 'Aghanim', detailing his role as Ganon's pawn and his goal to release Ganon. Following this, a 'key_ally' named 'Sahasrahla' is described as a wise guide. Finally, the 'stakes' for the movie are clearly defined as the 'total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon'. This slide completes the detailed foundational character and conflict setup within the `premise.json` file, following its introduction and initial content in the preceding slides.
slides.narration: Alright, so picking up right where we left off, after our main man Link, check out our 'key_antagonist' here: Aghanim! He's, like, Ganon's pawn, right? And his whole plan is to seal those seven maidens to, uh, release Ganon. Super villain stuff, bro. But fear not, we've got our 'key_ally,' Sahasrahla, the wise village elder, whose job it is to guide Link. And the 'stakes'? Oh man, they're massive! We're talking 'total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon.' This, folks, really completes all the essential building blocks in our `premise.json`. It's all in there, ready for the next agent, right?

slides.description: This slide is titled "Arc Agent" in large, bold blue text, set against a light grey background. It outlines the functionality of the next agent in the screenplay generation process, which is a chat agent designed to discuss the movie's story arc. The slide explains that the arc consists of multiple acts and that this agent takes the previously generated "premise.json" file as input for context. Users are instructed to tell the agent what happens in each act and then ask it to draft them one at a time. The slide concludes by stating that this agent will create an "arc.json" file. A small, cropped image of the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot is visible in the top right corner, consistent with the lecture's visual branding. This slide directly follows the detailed output of the Premise Agent and is the next step in the "Screenplay Flow" diagram.
slides.narration: Okay, so now that we've got our super detailed `premise.json` all sorted, right? The very next step in our awesome agentic flow is this guy: the Arc Agent. This is another one of those chat agents, folks, where you get to dive deep and discuss the *story arc* of your movie. You know, like, how it's gonna unfold across all the different acts. And here's the cool part: it takes our `premise.json` as input, so it already knows all that foundational stuff. You basically just tell it what's happening in each act, and it'll draft them for you, one at a time. Super efficient, right? And once you're done, guess what? It spits out that crucial `arc.json` file for us.

slides.description: This slide is titled "Arc.json" in large, bold blue text, set against a light grey background. It displays a dark code block containing a JSON file representing the story arc, specifically detailing 'Act 3'. This act is labeled "Battle Aghanim and the Dark World" and is noted to encompass 20-30 pages of a 120-page screenplay, serving as a climax and cliffhanger. The JSON includes a "dramatic_question", a comprehensive "summary" outlining Link's quest to defeat Aghanim and rescue Zelda, a "turning_point_end" where Aghanim banishes Link, and several "must_hit_beats" detailing specific plot points. This slide directly showcases the output file generated by the 'Arc Agent' introduced in the previous slide, demonstrating how the high-level story arc for the movie is structured into acts with detailed summaries and key plot points.
slides.narration: Okay, so here it is, folks! Our `arc.json` file, right? This is the output from our Arc Agent, specifically showing Act 3. Check out the label: "Battle Aghanim and the Dark World," and it even gives you an `approx_pages_note` – 20 to 30 pages of a 120-page screenplay, a climax *and* a cliffhanger! Pretty intense, huh? You've got your `dramatic_question` here, which is like, the core question of the act. And then, wow, look at that `summary` – it's super detailed, laying out Link's whole journey for this act. And we even have a `turning_point_end` and `must_hit_beats` that define those critical plot points. This is how we're really structuring that high-level story across acts, ensuring everything makes sense. Pretty cool, right?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Sequence Agent" in large bold blue text, describes the functionality of the next automated agent in the screenplay generation process. Against a light grey background, it explains that this agent is automated, unlike previous chat-based agents, and is responsible for writing the movie sequences for each act. The slide details that the agent takes `premise.json` and `arc.json` as inputs for global context, and also uses all previously generated sequences for local context to avoid drift. It outlines the primary elements a sequence must capture, including its narrative goal, key locations, and exit beat. Finally, the slide states that this agent will create a `sequence.json` file, completing another step in the hierarchical flow. A small, cropped image of the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot is present in the top right corner.
slides.narration: Okay, so now that we've got our `arc.json` all buttoned up, right, with all those acts defined? The *next* step in our amazing agentic flow is this, folks: the Sequence Agent. And here's where it gets really interesting, because this one is an *automated* agent. No chat needed, bro! It's gonna write all the sequences for each act of your movie. And listen, for its inputs, it's not just taking `premise.json` and `arc.json` for the global view, it's *also* taking all the previous sequences generated so far. Why? To give it that crucial *local context* so we don't get that pesky context drift, you know? It'll capture the goal of the sequence, the key locations, and the exit beat. And finally, yeah, it creates our `sequence.json` file. Super cool.

slides.description: This slide is titled "Sequence.json" in large bold blue text, set against a light grey background. It presents a dark code block displaying a JSON file, which is the output of the Sequence Agent. The file details the first movie sequence, labeled "SEQ1" for 'Act 1', with the title "Zelda's Urgent Plea." The JSON elaborates on the sequence's narrative "goal," its "emotional_tone" as urgent and sorrowful, and lists "key_locations" such as "Link's Home" and "Hyrule Castle (Outer Walls)." It concludes with a clear "exit_beat" describing Link arming himself to infiltrate Hyrule Castle. This slide directly demonstrates the detailed output of the Sequence Agent described in the previous slide, showcasing how the overall story arc is broken down into granular, actionable sequences with defined goals and beats, progressing the 'agentic flow' for screenplay generation.
slides.narration: Alright, so here it is, folks! Our glorious `sequence.json` file, right? This is the output from our Sequence Agent. And check it out, we're looking at "SEQ1" for Act 1, titled "Zelda's Urgent Plea." The *goal* here, like, wow, is to establish Link's call to adventure, his initial motivation – saving his uncle and Zelda – and getting his first tools. The `emotional_tone` is super clear: urgent, sorrowful, determined. And look at those `key_locations`: Link's Home, Hyrule Castle. And that `exit_beat`? Armed with his sword and shield, Link steels himself, ready to infiltrate the formidable Hyrule Castle. See how we're breaking down that high-level arc into these really specific, actionable sequences? Pretty powerful, right?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Scene Agent" in large bold blue text, describes the final automated component in the screenplay generation flow. Against a light grey background, it explains that this agent operates without a chat interface, writing individual scenes for each sequence of the movie. It takes `premise.json`, `arc.json`, and `sequence.json` as inputs for a global view, while also using all previously generated scenes within the current and previous sequences for crucial local context. The primary scene elements it generates include 'slug lines' for location and 'blocks' for actions and dialogues. Finally, this agent creates a `scenes.json` file, completing the hierarchical breakdown of the screenplay. A small, cropped image of the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot is visible in the top right corner, consistent with the lecture's branding.
slides.narration: Okay, so we've got our `sequence.json` all buttoned up, right? Now, for the really, really granular stuff, this, folks, is the Scene Agent. And guess what? This one is *also* an automated agent, no chat needed, bro! It's gonna be doing the heavy lifting of actually writing all those individual scenes for each sequence of your movie. And for its inputs, just like our Sequence Agent, it's taking `premise.json`, `arc.json`, and `sequence.json` to get that global view, right? But here's the absolute kicker for maintaining coherence: it *also* considers all the scenes generated so far within the current sequence, and even the previous sequence! That's how we keep that crucial *local context* flowing, so we don't get any weird context drift, you know? It'll generate those 'slug lines' – like, the location of the scene – and then the 'blocks' of actions and dialogues. And yeah, you guessed it, this agent ultimately creates our `scenes.json` file. Pretty cool, huh?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Scenes.json" in large bold blue text, showcases the final output of the Scene Agent, displayed as a dark code block against a light grey background. It presents the beginning of the `scenes.json` file, detailing the first scene of the screenplay. The scene, identified as "S0001" and "SEQ1-01," has a slugline "INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT" and is part of "SEQ1." The JSON structure includes an array of "blocks," outlining actions and dialogue. The scene opens with Link sleeping, followed by a whispered plea from Zelda (V.O.) and Link's disoriented awakening with visions of Hyrule Castle and Aghanim. This slide directly illustrates how the hierarchical breakdown of the screenplay, from premise to sequence, culminates in detailed, actionable scenes, providing a concrete example of the "scenes.json" file generated by the Scene Agent described in the previous slide.
slides.narration: Alright, so here it is, folks! The `scenes.json` file, right? This is the actual output from our Scene Agent – the really, really granular stuff. Look at this, it's our very first scene! We've got the `id`, `slug_id`, and that classic `slugline`: "INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT." And then, check out those `blocks` – this is where the action and dialogue come to life. Link's, like, sleeping, then Zelda's voice whispers, and he, you know, wakes up disoriented with visions of Aghanim. This is it, people! This is how that whole hierarchical breakdown culminates into actual, playable scenes for your movie. Pretty epic, right?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Enhancement Agents" in large bold blue text, introduces a method to improve the screenplay generation process. Against a light grey background, a main bullet point explains that the agentic flow can be enhanced by using agents to refine intermediate outputs, with sub-points suggesting to "Write better sequences or scenes," "Clean up the premise," and "Make the arc more creative." A golden robot labeled "ENHANCEMENT AGENT" with glowing blue eyes is depicted to the right of the text. At the bottom, a modified version of the "Screenplay Flow" diagram visually represents this concept: small robot icons, representing enhancement agents, are placed on the arrows connecting the "Premise," "Arc," "Sequences," and "Scenes" nodes, indicating that these agents act on the output of each stage before it progresses to the next. This slide builds upon the previous ones by proposing an iterative refinement step to optimize the quality of the generated screenplay elements, moving beyond initial content generation to improvement.
slides.narration: Okay, so we've got our `scenes.json`, right? We've generated a full screenplay, which is pretty awesome. But here's the thing, folks, the first draft is rarely perfect, right? So, this slide introduces "Enhancement Agents." We can actually *improve* this agentic flow, like, *significantly*, by adding agents that refine those intermediate outputs. You know, you can have an agent that writes *better* sequences or scenes, or one that helps you clean up the *premise* if it's not quite right. Maybe another one to make the *arc* more creative, you know? And if you look at the diagram below, you can see those little robot icons on the arrows between our Premise, Arc, Sequences, and Scenes. Those are our enhancement agents, basically, like, fine-tuning things before they get passed along. Pretty neat, huh?

slides.description: This slide is titled "Generalizing to Other Documents" in large, bold blue text, set against a light grey background. It highlights the broader applicability of the agentic flow beyond screenplays with a bullet point stating: "By modifying the agent prompts, this hierarchical flow can be used for other long text documents." The main content is a table demonstrating how the core components of the screenplay flow (Premise, Arc, Sequence, Scene) map to analogous elements in different document types. Examples include "Book" (Premise, Table of contents, Chapter, Sub-chapter), "Podcast" (Concept, Flow/format, Segment, Beat), "Song" (Premise, Structure, Verse, Couplet), "Lecture" (Learning objective, Outline, Module, Slide), and "Business plan" (Value prop, Outline, Section, Sub-section). Each document type is accompanied by a small "Artist Rendering" image depicting a robot interacting with that specific document, reinforcing the concept visually. This slide fulfills the promise made earlier in the lecture (Slide 2) that the agentic flow architecture is generalizable, effectively concluding the discussion by showing its versatility across various long-form content creations.
slides.narration: Okay, so we just talked about how we can enhance our flow with enhancement agents, right? That's super cool for getting a really polished screenplay. But here's the *really* mind-blowing part, folks: this exact same agentic flow? It's not just for screenplays! By just, like, modifying those agent prompts and what they're *looking* for, you can use this hierarchical structure for a *ton* of other long text documents. Look at this table! For a book, our Premise is the Premise, the Arc becomes your Table of Contents, Sequences are Chapters, and Scenes are Sub-chapters. For a podcast, it's Concept, Flow/Format, Segment, and Beat. Even a song, right? Premise, Structure, Verse, Couplet. And for *this* lecture, actually, our learning objective is the Premise, the Outline is the Arc, Modules are Sequences, and each Slide is a Scene. See? It's super generalizable! Pretty awesome, huh?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Coding Session" in large bold blue text, serves as a practical summary and call to action, set against a light grey background. It outlines three activities: "Write a short screenplay using Awesome-O," "Add enhancement agents to the agentic flow," and "Modify Awesome-O for different document types." Below the bullet points, a diagram visually represents the "Screenplay Flow," showing interconnected nodes for "Premise," "Arc," "Sequences," and "Scenes." Small robot icons are prominently placed on the arrows connecting these nodes, specifically illustrating the integration of "Enhancement Agents" as introduced in Slide 16. This slide synthesizes the core concepts of AI screenplay generation, iterative refinement, and generalization discussed throughout the lecture, preparing for a hands-on application of the presented agentic flow.
slides.narration: Okay, so we just saw how super generalizable this agentic flow is, right? Like, that's just wild, folks! Now, it's time to actually put all this awesome theory into practice in our coding session. First up, you're gonna get to write a short screenplay using Awesome-O, which is gonna be, like, so cool to see your ideas come to life. Then, you know, we talked about enhancement agents? You're gonna try adding those to *your* agentic flow to make your outputs even better. And finally, the big one: modify Awesome-O to generate *different* document types, really test that generalization we just talked about. See those little robots on the diagram? Those are your enhancement agents, folks. Get in there, experiment, and make some awesome stuff!
